chunks:
# ==============================================================================
# Chapter 18: Scaling IVD Across Your Organization
# ==============================================================================
# Module-level intent for the eighteenth chapter of the IVD book. The
# organizational scaling chapter in Part IV: moving IVD from one team to many
# teams. Covers pilot programs, training, recipe libraries, code review
# integration, CI/CD automation, metrics dashboards, and a real-world case
# study. Prose leads; examples illustrate.
#
# Reference: book/book_system_intent.yaml (system)
# Parent:    book_structure.part_4_adoption.chapters[1]
# Source:    framework.md, recipes/, ivd_system_intent.yaml
# Version: 1.0
# Created: 2026-02-09
# Status: Pending
# ==============================================================================

parent_intent: "../../../book_system_intent.yaml"

scope:
  level: "module"
  type: "chapter"
  granularity: "coarse"

# ==============================================================================
# INTENT: What should this chapter accomplish?
# ==============================================================================

intent:
  summary: "Guide the reader through scaling IVD from a single team to an organization — proving value with a pilot, enabling multiple teams through training, building recipe libraries, integrating IVD into code review and CI/CD, measuring adoption ROI, and grounding everything in a real-world case study."

  goal: |
    By the end of this chapter, the reader:
    1. Knows how to run a pilot team engagement that proves IVD's value before broader rollout
    2. Can design a training program that enables multiple teams to adopt IVD independently
    3. Understands how to build and curate an organizational recipe library (team-specific and shared)
    4. Can integrate IVD into code review workflows (intent alignment checks during PR review)
    5. Can set up CI/CD automation that verifies intent-to-code alignment on every commit
    6. Has a metrics framework for tracking IVD adoption and measuring impact (ROI dashboard)
    7. Has seen a realistic case study of organizational scaling with concrete lessons learned
    
    This chapter bridges individual adoption (Ch17) and advanced patterns (Ch19). The reader transitions from "my team uses IVD" to "my organization uses IVD." Prose leads; examples illustrate.

  success_metric: |
    - Reader can plan and execute a pilot team engagement with clear success criteria
    - Reader can outline a training program for onboarding new teams to IVD
    - Reader understands how to curate organizational recipes (shared patterns, team customizations)
    - Reader can describe how IVD fits into code review (what to check, how to flag misalignment)
    - Reader can describe CI/CD integration points (constraint verification, coverage gates, drift detection)
    - Reader has a concrete metrics framework: what to measure, how to measure it, what "good" looks like
    - Reader has seen scaling challenges and solutions through the case study
    - Adoption/scaling checklist completed at end of chapter

  stakeholders:
    - "IVD book author (ma

ng program for onboarding new teams to IVD
    - Reader understands how to curate organizational recipes (shared patterns, team customizations)
    - Reader can describe how IVD fits into code review (what to check, how to flag misalignment)
    - Reader can describe CI/CD integration points (constraint verification, coverage gates, drift detection)
    - Reader has a concrete metrics framework: what to measure, how to measure it, what "good" looks like
    - Reader has seen scaling challenges and solutions through the case study
    - Adoption/scaling checklist completed at end of chapter

  stakeholders:
    - "IVD book author (manuscript)"
    - "book/book_system_intent.yaml (part_4 purpose: adopt and scale IVD in teams and organizations)"
    - "recipes/ (organizational recipe library concept)"
    - "framework.md (IVD principles, verification, brownfield workflow)"

# ==============================================================================
# DELIVERABLES: Required sections (from book_system_intent Ch18)
# ==============================================================================

deliverables:
  sections:
    - id: "18.1"
      title: "Pilot Team: Proving Value Before Scaling"
      required_content:
        - "Why pilot first: scaling without evidence creates organizational resistance; a pilot generates proof that IVD works in YOUR context"
        - "Selecting the pilot team: criteria for choosing the right team (motivated, representative project, enough complexity to show IVD value, not too politically fraught)"
        - "Defining pilot success criteria: what 'IVD works here' means in measurable terms (fewer rework cycles, faster onboarding of new AI sessions, verifiable constraint coverage)"
        - "Running the pilot: the team follows Ch17's adoption steps (ivd init, assess coverage, scaffold, implement, verify) on their actual project"
        - "Capturing results: before/after measurements, team feedback, specific wins and friction points"
        - "The pilot deliverable: a short internal report or presentation that makes the case for broader adoption — evidence, not evangelism"

    - id: "18.2"
      title: "Training Program: Enabling Multiple Teams"
      required_content:
        - "From pilot to program: the pilot team becomes the seed — their experience informs the training design"
        - "Training structure: what teams need to learn (IVD concepts from Part I, practical workflow from Ch17, recipes from Part II, organizational conventions)"
        - "Hands-on over slides: effective IVD training is workshop-based — teams work on their own codebase during training, not a toy example"
        - "The 'intent first' habit: the single most important behavior change — before writing code or asking AI, write (or have AI write) the intent artifact"
        - "Champions model: each team designates an IVD champion who drives adoption within their team and connects with other champions"
        - "Common training pitfalls: over-teaching theory before practice, using generic examples instead of the team's own code, not addressing skepticism directly"

    - id: "18.3"
      title: "Recipe Library: Your Organization's Bes

e IVD training is workshop-based — teams work on their own codebase during training, not a toy example"
        - "The 'intent first' habit: the single most important behavior change — before writing code or asking AI, write (or have AI write) the intent artifact"
        - "Champions model: each team designates an IVD champion who drives adoption within their team and connects with other champions"
        - "Common training pitfalls: over-teaching theory before practice, using generic examples instead of the team's own code, not addressing skepticism directly"

    - id: "18.3"
      title: "Recipe Library: Your Organization's Best Practices"
      required_content:
        - "What an organizational recipe library is: shared, curated collection of intent templates for common patterns your teams build (agents, workflows, APIs, services)"
        - "Seeding the library: start with IVD's built-in recipes (agent-classifier, workflow, etc.) and add organization-specific recipes as teams discover patterns"
        - "Recipe anatomy review: structure, constraints_template, implementation_pattern, verification_pattern — briefly recap for scaling context"
        - "Team-specific vs shared recipes: some recipes apply to one team's domain; others become organizational standards (e.g., 'how we build API services')"
        - "Curation process: who reviews and promotes recipes from team-specific to shared; quality criteria (tested, documented, used by 2+ teams)"
        - "Recipe evolution: recipes improve as more teams use them — feedback loops, versioning, deprecation"
        - "The compounding effect: each new recipe reduces the friction for the next team adopting IVD"

    - id: "18.4"
      title: "Code Review with IVD: Intent Alignment Checks"
      required_content:
        - "IVD changes what code review looks like: reviewers check not just code quality but intent alignment"
        - "The intent alignment question: 'Does this PR satisfy the constraints in the intent artifact? Does the intent still reflect what the code does?'"
        - "What to check in review: constraint coverage (are all constraints tested?), bidirectional alignment (intent ↔ code), scope creep (did implementation drift beyond intent?)"
        - "Adding intent review to PR templates: suggested PR template additions (link to intent artifact, constraint status, alignment notes)"
        - "Training reviewers: how to review intent artifacts (is the intent clear? are constraints executable? are tests mapped?)"
        - "When intent and code disagree: the resolution protocol — update intent if requirements changed, fix code if implementation drifted"

    - id: "18.5"
      title: "CI/CD Integration: Automated Verification"
      required_content:
        - "What CI/CD can automate: constraint test execution, intent coverage checks (ivd_assess_coverage in pipeline), drift detection (intent ↔ code freshness)"
        - "Constraint verification on every PR: run the test paths defined in each constraint; fail the build if constraints are violated"
        - "Coverage gates: optional quality gate that checks intent coverage for modified modules — 'if you changed this module, does

ee: the resolution protocol — update intent if requirements changed, fix code if implementation drifted"

    - id: "18.5"
      title: "CI/CD Integration: Automated Verification"
      required_content:
        - "What CI/CD can automate: constraint test execution, intent coverage checks (ivd_assess_coverage in pipeline), drift detection (intent ↔ code freshness)"
        - "Constraint verification on every PR: run the test paths defined in each constraint; fail the build if constraints are violated"
        - "Coverage gates: optional quality gate that checks intent coverage for modified modules — 'if you changed this module, does it have an intent?'"
        - "Drift detection: flag when code changes significantly but the intent artifact hasn't been updated (or vice versa)"
        - "Incremental adoption: start with constraint tests only, add coverage gates later, add drift detection when the team is ready"
        - "Example pipeline configuration: illustrative CI/CD step definitions (conceptual, not provider-specific) showing where IVD checks fit"
        - "What NOT to automate: intent quality is a human judgment — CI checks structure and constraints, humans check meaning and alignment"

    - id: "18.6"
      title: "Metrics Dashboard: Tracking ROI"
      required_content:
        - "Why measure: without metrics, IVD adoption is a belief system — with metrics, it's an engineering practice with demonstrable ROI"
        - "What to measure: intent coverage (% of modules with intents), constraint pass rate (% of constraint tests passing), adoption velocity (teams onboarded, intents created), rework reduction (before/after comparison)"
        - "How to measure: derive metrics from intent artifacts (coverage), CI/CD results (constraint pass rate), git history (adoption velocity), project tracking (rework)"
        - "What 'good' looks like: benchmarks are organization-specific, not universal — compare against your own baseline, not industry averages"
        - "Dashboard design: what to show leadership vs what to show teams — leadership sees ROI and trends; teams see coverage and constraint health"
        - "Avoiding vanity metrics: coverage percentage alone means nothing if intents are low quality — pair quantitative metrics with qualitative review"

    - id: "18.7"
      title: "Case Study: Scaling IVD to 50 Developers"
      required_content:
        - "Realistic organizational scaling scenario: a mid-size engineering organization (multiple teams, shared services, mixed tech stacks) adopts IVD"
        - "The journey: pilot team → training program → recipe library → code review integration → CI/CD → metrics — following the chapter's own structure"
        - "Specific challenges encountered: resistance from experienced developers, recipe conflicts between teams, CI/CD false positives, metric gaming"
        - "How challenges were resolved: concrete solutions, not hand-waving — what the organization actually did"
        - "Results: measurable improvements with explicit caveats (author experience or labeled composite, not unsourced statistics)"
        - "Lessons learned: what they would do differently, what surprised them, what advice

The journey: pilot team → training program → recipe library → code review integration → CI/CD → metrics — following the chapter's own structure"
        - "Specific challenges encountered: resistance from experienced developers, recipe conflicts between teams, CI/CD false positives, metric gaming"
        - "How challenges were resolved: concrete solutions, not hand-waving — what the organization actually did"
        - "Results: measurable improvements with explicit caveats (author experience or labeled composite, not unsourced statistics)"
        - "Lessons learned: what they would do differently, what surprised them, what advice they'd give to organizations starting the scaling journey"
        - "Source attribution: case study is labeled as author experience or composite scenario — never presented as an unnamed third-party study"

  manuscript:
    file: "chapter.md"
    required_elements:
      - "Key Points or Summary block at end (constraint: chapter_takeaways)"
      - "Key Points reflect book_system_intent Ch18 key_points: from one team to many, organizational recipes, code review, CI/CD, measuring adoption"
      - "Adoption/Scaling Checklist: step-by-step checklist for organizational scaling (constraint: checklist_included)"
      - "1–2 quotable lines where natural (constraint: quotable_principles)"
      - "Clear bridge to Chapter 19 (advanced patterns)"
      - "Case study in 18.7 with source attribution (constraint: author_sourced_case_study)"
      - "No timeline references (constraint: no_timeline_references)"

# ==============================================================================
# KEY POINTS (from book_system_intent Ch18; must be reflected in chapter)
# ==============================================================================

key_points:
  - "From one team to many teams — scaling IVD requires proof (pilot), enablement (training), and infrastructure (recipes, CI/CD, metrics)"
  - "Creating organizational recipes — shared, curated intent templates that capture your organization's best practices and compound adoption"
  - "IVD in code review and CI/CD — intent alignment becomes part of PR review; constraint verification becomes part of the build pipeline"
  - "Measuring adoption and impact — coverage, constraint pass rate, adoption velocity, rework reduction; compare against your own baseline"

# ==============================================================================
# CONSTRAINTS (from book intent; chapter-specific)
# ==============================================================================

constraints:
  - name: "chapter_takeaways"
    requirement: "Chapter ends with 'Key Points' or 'Summary' block and an adoption/scaling checklist"
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints.chapter_takeaways"
    validation: "chapter.md contains a ## Key Points or ## Summary section plus scaling checklist"
    test: "grep '## Key Points\\|## Summary' chapter.md returns at least 1 match; grep -i 'checklist\\|scaling checklist' chapter.md returns at least 1 match"

  - name: "quotable_principles"
    requirement: "1–2 quotable, memorable lines in chapter where natural"
    source: "book_system_intent.writing_sty

=====================

constraints:
  - name: "chapter_takeaways"
    requirement: "Chapter ends with 'Key Points' or 'Summary' block and an adoption/scaling checklist"
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints.chapter_takeaways"
    validation: "chapter.md contains a ## Key Points or ## Summary section plus scaling checklist"
    test: "grep '## Key Points\\|## Summary' chapter.md returns at least 1 match; grep -i 'checklist\\|scaling checklist' chapter.md returns at least 1 match"

  - name: "quotable_principles"
    requirement: "1–2 quotable, memorable lines in chapter where natural"
    source: "book_system_intent.writing_style.quotable_lines"
    validation: "Manual: at least one line is tweetable/memorable"
    test: "At least one bold standalone sentence in chapter.md works as a tweet/pull-quote"

  - name: "writing_style_part_4"
    requirement: "Prose leads; examples illustrate. Prose explains and generalizes. Examples (YAML, CI config, PR templates, metrics) support the narrative, not the other way around."
    source: "book_system_intent.writing_style.code_and_prose.part_1_3_4"
    validation: "Manual: prose drives each section; examples appear as illustrations within the prose narrative"
    test: "Each section begins with prose explanation (not a code block); examples follow prose context; no section is example-only without narrative framing"

  - name: "formatting_conventions"
    requirement: "Follow book_system_intent.writing_style.formatting_conventions: bold for key terms, italic for emphasis, code for paths/commands, paragraph structure."
    source: "book_system_intent.writing_style.formatting_conventions"
    validation: "Manual: formatting matches conventions"
    test: "Key terms bolded on first use; code font for file paths, commands, and tool names; no paragraph exceeds 10 sentences without subheading"

  - name: "checklist_included"
    requirement: "Chapter includes a concrete adoption/scaling checklist the reader can follow when scaling IVD across their organization"
    source: "book_system_intent.clarity_tactics (checklists)"
    validation: "chapter.md contains a numbered or bulleted checklist with actionable scaling steps"
    test: "grep -i 'checklist' chapter.md returns at least 1 match; checklist contains at least 8 actionable items covering pilot through metrics"

  - name: "author_sourced_case_study"
    requirement: "Section 18.7 case study must be sourced from author experience or explicitly labeled as a composite scenario. Never presented as an unnamed third-party study or unattributed real company."
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints (author credibility, no_unsourced_statistics)"
    validation: "Section 18.7 includes explicit source attribution (author experience, composite, or named with permission)"
    test: "Section 18.7 contains 'author experience' OR 'composite' OR 'based on' attribution text; no unattributed company names or anonymous references"

  - name: "master_intent_alignment"
    requirement: "Chapter content must not contradict ivd_system_intent.yaml or book_system_intent.yaml"
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints.master_intent_alignment"
    validation: "Manual revie

unattributed real company."
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints (author credibility, no_unsourced_statistics)"
    validation: "Section 18.7 includes explicit source attribution (author experience, composite, or named with permission)"
    test: "Section 18.7 contains 'author experience' OR 'composite' OR 'based on' attribution text; no unattributed company names or anonymous references"

  - name: "master_intent_alignment"
    requirement: "Chapter content must not contradict ivd_system_intent.yaml or book_system_intent.yaml"
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints.master_intent_alignment"
    validation: "Manual review: Ch18 aligns with framework principles, book structure, and Part IV purpose; no contradictions"
    test: "No principle, framework concept, or organizational claim in chapter.md contradicts ivd_system_intent.yaml or book_system_intent.yaml"

  - name: "no_timeline_references"
    requirement: "Zero timeline references in chapter content (days, weeks, months as durations or deadlines) unless in clearly illustrative examples"
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints.no_timeline_references"
    validation: "grep -i 'days|weeks|months|timeline' chapter.md (excluding illustrative examples) returns no matches"
    test: "rg -i '\\b(days|weeks|months|timeline|by day|by week|end of week|on day)\\b' chapter.md outside clearly illustrative example context returns 0 matches"

  - name: "no_unsourced_statistics"
    requirement: "No percentage claims, adoption statistics, or industry data without explicit source or caveat. Case study results must be marked as illustrative (author experience or composite)."
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints.no_unsourced_statistics"
    validation: "Any numerical claim includes source citation or is labeled as illustrative/author experience/composite"
    test: "grep -E '[0-9]+%' chapter.md — every match has adjacent source or 'illustrative'/'author experience'/'composite' caveat"

# ==============================================================================
# RATIONALE
# ==============================================================================

rationale:
  decision: "Chapter 18 covers organizational scaling — moving IVD from one team to many. Part IV sequence: Ch17 getting started (individual/team), Ch18 scaling (organization), Ch19 advanced patterns, Ch20 ecosystem, Ch21 future."

  reasoning: |
    - Part IV purpose: adopt and scale IVD in teams and organizations. Ch18 is the scaling chapter — the natural next step after a team has successfully adopted IVD (Ch17).
    - Pilot-first approach: organizational adoption without evidence creates resistance. A pilot team generates proof in YOUR context, not abstract claims from the book. This is the credibility foundation for scaling.
    - Training over mandates: IVD adoption works when teams understand and choose it, not when it's imposed. The training program section emphasizes hands-on workshops on real codebases, not slide decks.
    - Recipe library as compounding asset: each recipe reduces friction for the next team. This is the organizational multiplier that makes scaling sub-linear in effort.
    - Code review and CI

lly adopted IVD (Ch17).
    - Pilot-first approach: organizational adoption without evidence creates resistance. A pilot team generates proof in YOUR context, not abstract claims from the book. This is the credibility foundation for scaling.
    - Training over mandates: IVD adoption works when teams understand and choose it, not when it's imposed. The training program section emphasizes hands-on workshops on real codebases, not slide decks.
    - Recipe library as compounding asset: each recipe reduces friction for the next team. This is the organizational multiplier that makes scaling sub-linear in effort.
    - Code review and CI/CD: IVD becomes part of engineering workflow, not a separate practice. Intent alignment checks in review and constraint verification in CI make IVD self-reinforcing.
    - Metrics for sustainability: without measurement, adoption is faith-based. The metrics dashboard makes IVD's value visible to both teams and leadership, sustaining investment.
    - Case study for credibility: organizational scaling is hard to believe without a concrete example. The case study (18.7) grounds the chapter's recommendations in reality.

  evidence: "book/book_system_intent.yaml (part_4, Ch18 key_points, placeholder); framework.md (IVD principles, verification, recipes); recipes/ (recipe structure and examples)"
  date: "2026-02-09"
  stakeholder: "IVD book author"

# ==============================================================================
# ALTERNATIVES
# ==============================================================================

alternatives:
  - name: "Present organizational scaling as a prescriptive playbook with fixed phases and milestones"
    rejected_because: "Organizations vary enormously in size, structure, culture, and technical maturity. A prescriptive playbook would be wrong for most readers. The chapter provides a framework (pilot → train → recipes → review → CI/CD → metrics) that each organization adapts to their context."
    when_it_works: "If writing for a specific organizational archetype (e.g., 'enterprise with 500+ developers') — but the book's audience is broader."

  - name: "Skip the case study and rely on the chapter's framework alone"
    rejected_because: "Organizational scaling is where skepticism is highest — 'does this actually work at scale?' The case study (even as composite/author experience) grounds the framework in concrete reality. Without it, the chapter is theory about practice."
    when_it_works: "If the book had a separate 'Case Studies' appendix with multiple organizational examples — but Part IV is where the reader needs to see scaling evidence."

# ==============================================================================
# RISKS
# ==============================================================================

risks:
  - condition: "Chapter feels too prescriptive — readers in different organizational contexts can't apply the guidance"
    action: "Frame each section as a pattern with principles, not a fixed procedure. Emphasize adaptation: 'your pilot criteria will reflect your organization's priorities'; 'your recipe library starts with your most common patterns.'"
    monito

iple organizational examples — but Part IV is where the reader needs to see scaling evidence."

# ==============================================================================
# RISKS
# ==============================================================================

risks:
  - condition: "Chapter feels too prescriptive — readers in different organizational contexts can't apply the guidance"
    action: "Frame each section as a pattern with principles, not a fixed procedure. Emphasize adaptation: 'your pilot criteria will reflect your organization's priorities'; 'your recipe library starts with your most common patterns.'"
    monitor: "Beta reader feedback: 'this doesn't apply to my organization' or 'too rigid'"
    severity: "high"

  - condition: "Case study (18.7) feels contrived or fictional, undermining credibility"
    action: "Ground the case study in specific, realistic details — real challenges (developer resistance, CI false positives, metric gaming), real resolutions. Label clearly as author experience or composite. Include what went wrong, not just what went right."
    monitor: "Beta reader feedback: 'the case study felt made up' or 'too polished'"
    severity: "high"

  - condition: "CI/CD integration section becomes provider-specific and dates quickly"
    action: "Keep CI/CD examples conceptual and provider-agnostic. Show the integration pattern (where IVD checks fit in a pipeline), not specific GitHub Actions / GitLab CI / Jenkins syntax. Reference that teams adapt to their tooling."
    monitor: "Reader feedback post-publication: 'the CI examples don't match my setup'"
    severity: "medium"

  - condition: "Metrics section promotes vanity metrics that don't reflect actual IVD value"
    action: "Explicitly warn against vanity metrics (coverage % without quality, intent count without constraint pass rate). Pair quantitative metrics with qualitative review. Emphasize comparing against your own baseline, not industry benchmarks."
    monitor: "Beta reader feedback: 'we tracked coverage but it didn't help'"
    severity: "medium"
